nodes.title: Define a vector
nodes.brief: Recognise a vector as a quantity having both magnitude and direction.
nodes.quote: A quantity that has magnitude as well as direction is called a vector.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What makes a vector.
A quantity that has BOTH magnitude AND direction is called a vector; a quantity with magnitude alone is a scalar. Mass (5 kg) is a scalar; velocity (30 m/s north) is a vector.

PART 2 — Representing a vector as a directed line segment.
A vector is drawn as an arrow from an initial point A to a terminal point B, written AB (with an arrow); the length of the arrow is the magnitude and the arrowhead gives the direction. Example: if A is (0,0) and B is (3,4) the arrow from A to B represents a vector of magnitude 5 in the direction of B from A.

PART 3 — Distinguishing vector from scalar.
Check: does the quantity change meaning if you reverse its direction? If yes, it is a vector (force, displacement, velocity); if no, it is a scalar (speed, temperature, mass). Displacement 5 km east is NOT the same as 5 km west, confirming it is a vector.
keyMoves: Check the quantity has both a size and a direction
Represent it as a directed line segment (arrow)
Distinguish it from a scalar (magnitude only)
misconceptions: Treating a scalar (e.g. mass) as a vector
Confusing the initial and terminal points

nodes.title: Find the magnitude (length) of a vector
nodes.brief: Define the magnitude as the distance between the initial and terminal points.
nodes.quote: The distance between initial and terminal points of a vector is called the *magnitude* (or length) of the vector
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition of magnitude.
The magnitude (or length) of a vector a, written |a|, is the distance between its initial and terminal points. Magnitude is always a non-negative real number; it is zero only for the zero vector.

PART 2 — Computing magnitude from components.
For a vector a = x i + y j + z k, the magnitude is |a| = sqrt(x^2 + y^2 + z^2) by the 3-D distance formula. Example: a = 2 i + 3 j + 6 k gives |a| = sqrt(4 + 9 + 36) = sqrt(49) = 7.

PART 3 — Common traps.
Square EVERY component before adding — do not sum the components first, then square. Also note that |a| >= 0 always; a negative answer signals an arithmetic error.
keyMoves: Identify the components x, y, z of the vector
Apply the distance formula |a| = sqrt(x^2 + y^2 + z^2)
Report a non-negative length
misconceptions: Reporting a negative magnitude
Forgetting to square and add all components

nodes.title: Define the position vector of a point
nodes.brief: Recognise the position vector of P as the vector OP from the origin to P.
nodes.quote: is called the *position vector* of the point P with respect to O.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition.
For a point P(x, y, z) in 3-D space, the vector from the origin O(0,0,0) to P is called the position vector of P with respect to O, written OP = x i + y j + z k. The position vector pins the point to the coordinate system.

PART 2 — Magnitude and direction.
The magnitude of the position vector equals the distance from the origin: |OP| = sqrt(x^2 + y^2 + z^2). Example: P = (1, 2, 2) gives OP = i + 2j + 2k with |OP| = sqrt(1+4+4) = 3.

PART 3 — Vector between two points.
If A has position vector a and B has position vector b, the vector AB = b - a. This is the key link between coordinate geometry and vector algebra: AB = OB - OA.
keyMoves: Take the origin O as the initial point and P as the terminal point
Write the position vector as OP = x i + y j + z k
Compute its magnitude by the distance formula
misconceptions: Starting the position vector somewhere other than the origin
Confusing a position vector with a free vector between two arbitrary points

nodes.title: Find the direction cosines of a vector
nodes.brief: Identify the direction cosines l, m, n as the cosines of the angles with the axes.
nodes.quote: are called *direction cosines* of the vector
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition of direction angles and direction cosines.
A vector a makes angles alpha, beta, gamma with the positive x-, y-, z-axes respectively; these are its direction angles. Their cosines l = cos(alpha), m = cos(beta), n = cos(gamma) are the direction cosines. For a = a1 i + a2 j + a3 k with magnitude |a|: l = a1/|a|, m = a2/|a|, n = a3/|a|.

PART 2 — The identity l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1.
Because (a1^2 + a2^2 + a3^2)/|a|^2 = |a|^2/|a|^2 = 1, the direction cosines always satisfy l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1. The unit vector in the direction of a is therefore l i + m j + n k.

PART 3 — Worked example.
For a = 3 i + 4 j + 0 k, |a| = 5. Direction cosines: l = 3/5, m = 4/5, n = 0. Check: (9+16+0)/25 = 1. The direction ratios are just 3, 4, 0 (the raw components, not divided by the magnitude).
keyMoves: Identify the angles the vector makes with the x, y, z axes
Take their cosines l, m, n (= components divided by magnitude)
Check l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1
misconceptions: Confusing direction cosines (normalised) with direction ratios (the raw components)
Forgetting to divide each component by the magnitude

nodes.title: Recognise the zero (null) vector
nodes.brief: Identify the zero vector as one whose initial and terminal points coincide.
nodes.quote: A vector whose initial and terminal points coincide, is called a zero vector (or null vector)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition.
A vector whose initial and terminal points coincide is called the zero vector (or null vector), written 0 (bold). Its magnitude is 0 and its direction is indeterminate — it may be regarded as having any direction. The zero vector cannot be visualised as an arrow with a sense, but it is the essential additive identity.

PART 2 — Role as additive identity.
For any vector a: a + 0 = 0 + a = a. Also a - a = 0. This makes the zero vector the identity element for vector addition, just as 0 plays for ordinary addition. Example: displacement 5 km east then 5 km west returns you to the start — net displacement is the zero vector.

PART 3 — When the zero vector arises naturally.
The cross product of any vector with itself is the zero vector: a x a = 0. The zero vector also arises when a vector is scaled by the scalar 0: 0 * a = 0. Recognising it as a vector (not just the number zero) avoids confusion in later operations.
keyMoves: Check the initial and terminal points are the same
Recognise the magnitude is zero, direction indeterminate
Use it as the additive identity in vector addition
misconceptions: Assigning a fixed direction to the zero vector
Confusing the zero vector with a unit vector

nodes.title: Find the unit vector in a direction
nodes.brief: Recognise a unit vector as one of magnitude 1, and form it as a / |a|.
nodes.quote: A vector whose magnitude is unity (i.e., 1 unit) is called a unit vector.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition.
A vector whose magnitude is exactly 1 is called a unit vector. The unit vector in the direction of a non-zero vector a is denoted a-hat and equals a / |a|. The standard unit vectors along the coordinate axes are i (x-axis), j (y-axis), k (z-axis).

PART 2 — How to find a-hat.
Step 1: compute |a| = sqrt(x^2 + y^2 + z^2). Step 2: divide every component by |a|. Example: a = 1 i + 2 j + 2 k gives |a| = 3, so a-hat = (1/3) i + (2/3) j + (2/3) k. Verify: |(1/3)^2 + (2/3)^2 + (2/3)^2| = sqrt(1/9 + 4/9 + 4/9) = sqrt(9/9) = 1.

PART 3 — Why unit vectors matter.
Unit vectors carry pure direction with no magnitude information. In direction-cosine notation, a-hat = l i + m j + n k. Any vector a can be written a = |a| * a-hat, cleanly separating its magnitude and direction.
keyMoves: Compute the magnitude |a| of the given vector
Divide the vector by its magnitude: a-hat = a / |a|
Check the result has magnitude 1
misconceptions: Forgetting to divide by the magnitude when forming a unit vector
Thinking a unit vector has no direction

nodes.title: Recognise collinear vectors
nodes.brief: Identify collinear vectors as those parallel to the same line.
nodes.quote: Two or more vectors are said to be collinear if they are parallel to the same line, irrespective of their magnitudes and directions.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition.
Two or more vectors are collinear if they are all parallel to the same line, regardless of their magnitudes and directions. Collinear means the vectors lie along lines that are parallel (or the same line) — it does NOT require equal magnitude or the same sense.

PART 2 — Algebraic test.
Vectors a and b (b not 0) are collinear if and only if a = lambda * b for some scalar lambda. If lambda > 0 they point the same way; if lambda < 0 they point opposite ways; either satisfies collinearity. Example: a = 2 i + 4 j and b = i + 2 j are collinear because a = 2 b (lambda = 2).

PART 3 — Component test.
For a = (a1, a2, a3) and b = (b1, b2, b3), they are collinear iff a1/b1 = a2/b2 = a3/b3 (the common ratio is lambda). If any component of b is zero, the matching component of a must also be zero. Example: a = 3 i - 6 j + 9 k and b = i - 2 j + 3 k: 3/1 = -6/-2 = 9/3 = 3, so a = 3 b — collinear.
keyMoves: Check whether the vectors are parallel to one common line
Equivalently test a = lambda b for some scalar lambda
Ignore magnitude and sense when judging collinearity
misconceptions: Requiring collinear vectors to have equal magnitude or the same direction
Confusing collinear (parallel) with equal vectors

nodes.title: Recognise equal vectors
nodes.brief: Identify equal vectors as those with the same magnitude AND direction, regardless of position.
nodes.quote: Two vectors a and b are said to be equal, if they have the same magnitude and direction regardless of the positions of their initial points
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition.
Two vectors a and b are equal (written a = b) if and only if they have the same magnitude AND the same direction, regardless of where their initial points are located. We treat vectors as free vectors that can be translated parallel to themselves without becoming different vectors.

PART 2 — Component criterion.
In component form, a = a1 i + a2 j + a3 k equals b = b1 i + b2 j + b3 k if and only if a1 = b1, a2 = b2, and a3 = b3. This gives three simultaneous equations when we set two unknown vectors equal. Example: a = 2 i + 3 j - k equals b only if b = 2 i + 3 j - k, whatever the initial point of b.

PART 3 — Equality vs collinearity.
Equal vectors are always collinear (with lambda = 1), but collinear vectors are not necessarily equal (they may differ in magnitude or sense). Two equal vectors placed at different positions in space are the same free vector — the defining property of the free-vector model used throughout this chapter.
keyMoves: Compare both magnitude and direction of the two vectors
Ignore where their initial points sit (free vectors)
Declare them equal only if both match
misconceptions: Calling vectors equal just because they have equal magnitude
Thinking equal vectors must share the same initial point

nodes.title: Add vectors by the triangle and parallelogram laws
nodes.brief: Add two vectors head-to-tail (triangle law) or as adjacent sides of a parallelogram.
nodes.quote: This is known as the *parallelogram law of vector addition*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Triangle law.
To add vectors a and b, place the tail of b at the head of a. The vector from the tail of a to the head of b is a + b. This is the triangle law (or head-to-tail rule). Example: a = 3 i and b = 4 j gives a + b = 3 i + 4 j with |a + b| = 5.

PART 2 — Parallelogram law.
Alternatively, draw a and b from the same initial point as two adjacent sides of a parallelogram. The diagonal from that initial point is a + b. This is the parallelogram law of vector addition. Both laws give the same resultant.

PART 3 — Properties of vector addition.
Vector addition is commutative (a + b = b + a), associative ((a+b)+c = a+(b+c)), and has the zero vector as identity (a + 0 = a). The negative of a is -a (same magnitude, opposite direction), and a - b = a + (-b). In components: (a1 i + a2 j + a3 k) + (b1 i + b2 j + b3 k) = (a1+b1) i + (a2+b2) j + (a3+b3) k.
keyMoves: Join the vectors head-to-tail for the triangle law
Or take them as adjacent sides; the diagonal is the sum
Use commutativity/associativity to simplify
misconceptions: Adding the magnitudes instead of the vectors
Joining the vectors tail-to-tail when applying the triangle law

nodes.title: Write a vector in component form
nodes.brief: Express a vector as x i + y j + z k with scalar components x, y, z.
nodes.quote: This form of any vector is called its *component form*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Component form definition.
Any vector a in 3-D space can be written as a = x i + y j + z k, where i, j, k are unit vectors along the x-, y-, z-axes and x, y, z are the scalar components. This is the component form (rectangular form) of a. The vector components are x i, y j, z k (each a vector along one axis).

PART 2 — Operations in component form.
Addition: (x1 i + y1 j + z1 k) + (x2 i + y2 j + z2 k) = (x1+x2) i + (y1+y2) j + (z1+z2) k. Scalar multiplication: lambda * (x i + y j + z k) = (lambda x) i + (lambda y) j + (lambda z) k. Magnitude: |a| = sqrt(x^2 + y^2 + z^2).

PART 3 — Finding components from two points.
If a vector goes from point A(x1, y1, z1) to B(x2, y2, z2), its component form is AB = (x2-x1) i + (y2-y1) j + (z2-z1) k. Example: A = (1,2,3), B = (4,0,5): AB = 3 i - 2 j + 2 k, |AB| = sqrt(9+4+4) = sqrt(17).
keyMoves: Resolve the vector along the i, j, k directions
Write it as x i + y j + z k
Operate componentwise for addition and scalar multiplication
misconceptions: Confusing the scalar components (x, y, z) with the vector components (x i, y j, z k)
Forgetting the unit vectors i, j, k when writing component form

nodes.title: Apply the section formula for a dividing point
nodes.brief: Find the position vector of a point dividing a segment in a given ratio, internally or externally.
nodes.quote: the position vector of the point R which divides P and Q internally in the ratio of *m* : *n* is given by
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Internal division.
If P and Q have position vectors p and q, the point R that divides PQ internally in the ratio m : n has position vector r = (m q + n p) / (m + n). R lies between P and Q; m and n are both positive. Example: P has position vector i + 2 j, Q has position vector 4 i + 5 j, ratio 1:2 internally. r = (1*(4i+5j) + 2*(i+2j)) / 3 = (4i+5j+2i+4j)/3 = (6i+9j)/3 = 2i + 3j.

PART 2 — Midpoint and external division.
Midpoint (m = n): r = (p + q)/2. External division in ratio m : n (R outside PQ, m not= n): r = (m q - n p)/(m - n). In external division R is not between P and Q.

PART 3 — Mnemonic for internal division.
The formula is a weighted average: R is closer to Q if m is larger. Remember: the NUMERATOR pairs m with q and n with p (the OPPOSITE endpoint). Writing it as r = (m q + n p)/(m+n) helps avoid the common swap error.
keyMoves: Identify the ratio m : n and whether division is internal or external
Apply (m q + n p)/(m + n) internally, (m q - n p)/(m - n) externally
Use (p + q)/2 for the midpoint
misconceptions: Swapping which point goes with m and which with n
Using the internal formula for external division

nodes.title: Compute the scalar (dot) product
nodes.brief: Use a.b = |a||b|cos(theta); equivalently a1 b1 + a2 b2 + a3 b3.
nodes.quote: The scalar product of two nonzero vectors a and b, denoted by a.b, is
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition.
The scalar (dot) product of two non-zero vectors a and b is a.b = |a| |b| cos(theta), where theta is the angle between them (0 <= theta <= pi). If either vector is the zero vector, a.b = 0 by convention. The result is a SCALAR (a number), not a vector.

PART 2 — Component formula and key facts.
In component form: a.b = a1 b1 + a2 b2 + a3 b3. Perpendicularity test: a.b = 0 iff a and b are perpendicular (theta = 90 deg, cos 90 = 0). Angle formula: cos(theta) = a.b / (|a| |b|). Example: a = 1 i + 2 j + 2 k, b = 3 i + 0 j + 4 k. a.b = 3+0+8 = 11. |a| = 3, |b| = 5. cos(theta) = 11/15.

PART 3 — Projection and properties.
The projection of a onto b is (a.b)/|b|. Properties: commutative (a.b = b.a), distributive over addition (a.(b+c) = a.b + a.c), and a.a = |a|^2. Standard basis: i.i = j.j = k.k = 1; i.j = j.k = k.i = 0.
keyMoves: Use a.b = |a||b| cos(theta) or a1 b1 + a2 b2 + a3 b3
Note the result is a scalar (a number)
Use a.b = 0 to test perpendicularity, and cos(theta) = a.b/(|a||b|) for the angle
misconceptions: Expecting the dot product to be a vector
Forgetting the cos(theta) factor or summing only some component products

nodes.title: Compute the vector (cross) product
nodes.brief: Use a x b = |a||b| sin(theta) n-hat; it is a vector perpendicular to both, anticommutative.
nodes.quote: The vector product of two nonzero vectors a and b, is denoted by a x b and defined as
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition.
The vector (cross) product of two non-zero vectors a and b is a x b = |a| |b| sin(theta) n-hat, where theta is the angle between them (0 <= theta <= pi) and n-hat is the unit vector perpendicular to both such that a, b, n-hat form a right-handed system. The result is a VECTOR, unlike the dot product.

PART 2 — Component formula (determinant expansion).
For a = a1 i + a2 j + a3 k and b = b1 i + b2 j + b3 k:
a x b = (a2 b3 - a3 b2) i - (a1 b3 - a3 b1) j + (a1 b2 - a2 b1) k.
Example: a = 1 i + 2 j + 3 k, b = 4 i + 5 j + 6 k.
a x b = (2*6 - 3*5) i - (1*6 - 3*4) j + (1*5 - 2*4) k = (12-15) i - (6-12) j + (5-8) k = -3 i + 6 j - 3 k.

PART 3 — Key properties.
Anticommutative: a x b = -(b x a). Self-cross-product: a x a = 0 (zero vector). Magnitude = area of parallelogram with sides a and b: |a x b| = |a||b| sin(theta). Standard basis: i x j = k, j x k = i, k x i = j (and reverses give negatives). If a x b = 0 and both are non-zero, then sin(theta) = 0 so a and b are parallel (collinear).
keyMoves: Use a x b = |a||b| sin(theta) n-hat (a vector perpendicular to both)
Compute it as the i, j, k determinant in component form
Apply anticommutativity a x b = -(b x a) and |a x b| = parallelogram area
misconceptions: Treating the cross product as a scalar like the dot product
Assuming a x b = b x a (it is anticommutative)
